title: "The Population of Migrant Laborers in Renaissance Tuscany" by William J. Connell
highlights: years ago I discovered in the Florentine State Archives the records of a tax that was imposed on foreign workers in the year 1473, at the height of the Florentine Renaissance. The tax was applied to foreigners working both in the city of Florence and throughout the 44 jurisdictions of the Florentine state in Tuscany. The law’s preamble complained that the manual laborers who were coming from outside Florentine territory were saving their wages and sending them home rather than contributing to the local economy. So Florence’s ruling magistrates established a system whereby each manual laborer who was not a native would be required to pay an annual tax. In exchange the worker would receive a numbered worker’s visa, valid for one year. If he
